title: Emitting Jobs
description: Fire-and-forget, awaitable, and blocking emission patterns

blocks.text: Hound exposes three emission methods depending on how much you need to know about a job's fate:

rows: Job to be written to storage
Job to reach a terminal state

blocks.text: The simplest form. Returns the jobId synchronously and writes to storage in the background:
Because the write is async, the job may not be in storage yet when emit() returns. Use emitAsync() if you need to confirm it was persisted.
Awaits both the state key and queue write before resolving. Use this when you need to confirm the job exists in storage before continuing:

const jobId = await hound.emitAsync('order.process', { orderId: '123' });
console.log('Job persisted:', jobId);
Emits a job and waits for it to reach a terminal state (completed or failed). Returns the final JobRecord. Rejects on failure or timeout:
emitAndWait() defaults to a 30-second timeout. Pass timeoutMs to override.
All three emission methods accept an options object as the third argument:

rows: Schedule the job for a future time
Lower number = higher priority within the queue
